Rautenburghs Trompe l'œil with letters Estimate: 6,000 - 8,000 GBP Italian, Sienese, 15th century Figure of the Virgin Estimate: 5,000 - 7,000 USD Bolognese School, early 17th century Portrait of a boy, bust-length, in a white ruff Estimate: 8,000 - 12,000 GBP Charles Théodore Frère Encampment in the desert Estimate: 40,000 - 60,000 GBP Charles James Theriat The Caravan Estimate: 30,000 - 50,000 GBP Nasreddine (Etienne) Dinet A Captive Audience Estimate: 50,000 - 70,000 GBP Originally from the storied collection of Christiane and Antonin Besse who lovingly built a collection of South Arabian sculpture while living in the then British colony of Aden in the middle 20 th century (see ), this sculpture stands as a compelling testament to the religious life, social structure, and artistic sophistication of ancient South Arabia, offering rare insight into a culture whose visual traditions remain less familiar than those of its contemporaries, yet equally rich and expressive. This lot has been withdrawn Lot Details Description A South Arabian Alabaster Figure of a Woman, Qataban 3rd Century B.C./1st Century A.D. Origins II Origins II A South Arabian Alabaster Figure of a Woman, Qataban, 3rd Century B.C./1st Century A.D. Carved from translucent calcite-alabaster, a material prized for its luminous qualities, the figure displays the stylized aesthetic characteristic of South Arabian figurative art: a frontal pose, simplified bodily forms, and an emphasis on facial presence and symmetry. Originally, such figures may have featured painted details or inlaid facial detailing, such as extant here in the figures recessed eyes, enhancing their lifelike presence and ritual impact. text_for_matching_web: Standing male figure ca. late 1st millennium BCE Not on view This standing male figure is a funerary or votive object intended to be placed in a tomb or temple. Dressed in a long, plain garment reaching almost to the ankles, the figure stands in a fully frontal pose. The base, which is carved in one piece with the figure, bears the name of the individual and his clan—Sadiqim Ma'ad. The eyes and eyebrows are carved to hold inlays, now missing, and the arms are bent at the elbows, extending out at right angles to the body. One hand is clenched in a fist, pierced vertically to hold some object, perhaps a scepter, now lost. The other hand is open. The undersurface of the base and the top and back of the figure's head are roughly carved. Such statues were often set in niches, and the surfaces not visible were consequently left unfinished on many examples. The placement of the ears high on the head, the aquiline nose, the small mouth, and the fully frontal pose are all characteristic of human images made in this region. wb_txt_shrt: Standing male figure, alabaster (calcite), ca. late 1st millennium BCE. Funerary or votive object carved in a fully frontal pose, with a long plain garment and a base inscribed with the name Sadiqim Ma'ad.
wb_txt_lg: Standing male figure, alabaster (calcite), ca. late 1st millennium BCE. This funerary or votive object was intended for placement in a tomb or temple. The figure stands frontally and wears a long, plain garment reaching almost to the ankles. It is carved in one piece with a base that bears the name of the individual and his clan, Sadiqim Ma'ad. The eyes and eyebrows are carved to receive inlays, now missing. The arms are bent at the elbows and extend outward at right angles to the body one hand is clenched in a fist and pierced vertically to hold an object, perhaps a scepter, now lost, while the other hand is open. The undersurface of the base and the top and back of the head are roughly carved, consistent with statues that were set in niches and left unfinished on non-visible surfaces. The ears are placed high on the head, and the aquiline nose, small mouth, and fully frontal pose are characteristic of human images made in southwestern Arabia.

text_for_matching_web: Incense burner ca. mid-1st millennium BCE Not on view This bronze incense burner from southwestern Arabia consists of a cylindrical cup set on a conical base. A rectangular architectural form suggesting a facade extends upward from the cup's front. Its face is decorated with two serpents flanking a round disk set within a crescent, all in low relief. Cast separately, an ibex standing on a plinth projects from the front of the incense burner and may serve as a handle. From the middle of the first millennium B.C. until the sixth century A.D., the kingdoms of southwestern Arabia gained considerable wealth and power through their control of the trade in incense between Arabia and the lands of the Mediterranean seacoast. Frankincense and myrrh, gum resins that are native to southern Arabia, were widely valued in the ancient world for the preparation of incense, perfumes, cosmetics, and medicines, as well as for use in religious and funerary ceremonies. The importance of incense in the religion of southwestern Arabia is reflected in this object
wb_txt_shrt: Incense burner, bronze, from southwestern Arabia, ca. mid-1st millennium BCE. It has a cylindrical cup on a conical base, with a rectangular architectural facade rising from the front, decorated in low relief with two serpents flanking a round disk within a crescent. A separately cast ibex on a plinth projects from the front and may have served as a handle.
wb_txt_lg: Incense burner, bronze, from southwestern Arabia, dated to ca. mid-1st millennium BCE. The vessel consists of a cylindrical cup set on a conical base, with a rectangular architectural form rising from the front of the cup and suggesting a facade. The facade is decorated in low relief with two serpents flanking a round disk set within a crescent. A separately cast ibex standing on a plinth projects from the front of the burner and may have functioned as a handle. The object is associated with the importance of incense in the religion of southwestern Arabia.

text_for_matching_web: Standing bull ca. mid- to late 1st millennium BCE Not on view This bronze bull stands on a double-based plinth. Its head is strongly sculpted with upright ears behind horns that curve up and slightly inward. Heavy brows arch over the eyes, which are inlaid with shell. The tail hangs straight down to the hooves and parallels those of southwestern Arabian statues of standing bulls sculpted in alabaster, suggesting that this piece dates to the late first millennium B.C. like the alabaster bulls. By the middle of the first millennium B.C., kingdoms had emerged in southwestern Arabia whose power was based on control of the trade in frankincense and myrrh, which are native to the region. The kingdoms' immense wealth is reflected in the use of bronze to cast large sculptures, as well as smaller objects, which were produced throughout most of the first millennium B.C. Bulls are commonly represented and can be found on funerary stelae, seals, and sculpture. Read more This striking head is a grave marker or perhaps a commemorative piece from the ancient kingdom of Saba which ruled over the lands of southwestern Arabia, centered in modern day Yemen. The wealth of the kingdom is legendary, and is primarily attributable to Saba's position at the crossroads of the ancient world, receiving consignments (particularly of incense) from all across the Middle East, Asia and the Mediterranean basin. Technically, the Sabeans were one of four major powers in this area, also including the Minaeans, the Qatabanians and the Hadramites, but the peoples as a whole became subsumed as a single entity by the middle of the first millennium BC. This piece depicts a feminine head and neck, and appears to have once served as a grave marker, or perhaps as part of a larger alabaster structure. Their religion and mythology fuelled the themes of their sculptural works – primarily anthropomorphic and zoomorphic statuary – while their contact with other cultures and nations led to a highly derived and distinctive style.

At the beginning of each Ner, or chapter, there is red and black ornamentation, with the exception of the sixth and seventh Ners. The title page is illustrated with a two-colour frame and states that the book Menorat HaMaor was written by Rabbi Yitzchak Abuhav the Sephardi and written here in Sana'a in 1848. The text notes that Menorat HaMaor was widely embraced by Yemenite Jews and read in synagogues on Shabbat afternoon.
pdf_upgrd_description: Hebrew manuscript on paper, dated 1848, comprising 227 leaves and written in two columns per page with red ink headings and dialects. The manuscript includes red and black decorative elements at the beginning of each Nir, except Nirs VI and VII, and a framed title page (blonin) bearing the inscription that the book was written in Sana'a in 1848. Copies of the writer appear at the end of the book. It is described as a complete manuscript of formation, lacking a number of index leaves, and predating the well-known Livorno edition. Condition is noted as good.

wb_txt_shrt: Female votive figure, marble with gesso-painted surface, standing frontally on a small rectangular integral plinth and dated to circa 1st century B.C. The figure wears a long dress and holds a rectangular hollowed-out votive bowl.
wb_txt_lg: Female votive figure, marble with an applied gesso layer painted with coloured details, standing frontally on a small rectangular integral plinth and dated to circa 1st century B.C. The figure wears a long dress, waisted and ending at the calves above ankle bracelets, with incised linear and cross-hatched ornament over the legs and around the upper arms. She is shown with strands of necklaces composed of triangular pendants and a large central crescentic pendant above the breasts, and her arms are bent at the elbows with the forearms projecting forward to hold a rectangular hollowed-out votive bowl with incised panels. The oval face has a pointed chin, strong browline, incised arched brows, large eyes with drilled pupils, a straight nose, and a small mouth. The hair flares below the ears and falls to the shoulders, with incised linear banded details and a diadem of circular pendants above the forehead. Height: 31 in (79 cm).
